Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually dedicating time in the evenings and on weekends to go through readings and notes. With 27 hours a week, I spread things out across multiple days, though I'll admit some weeks felt more scattered than others. I'm a read-write learner, so I spent a lot of time typing up notes and summarizing materials in my own words. Even when I wasn't feeling particularly motivated, I'd force myself to at least sit down and review something.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. I'd try to maintain some regularity, but honestly, when deadlines approached or I had multiple assignments due, everything got more intense. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, other weeks I'd barely look at anything until pressure built up. My attendance wasn't great at around 58%, so I compensated by putting in more independent study time when I could.
Q3: Usually it was either checking for new announcements, seeing if grades were posted, or accessing specific readings I needed. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because there was something almost addictive about completing those challenges and seeing my progress. The anxiety of missing something important also pushed me to log in frequently, even when I wasn't planning to study deeply.
Q4: Yes, checking the LMS became almost automatic, especially first thing in the morning with my coffee. I think the gamified elements really helped build that habit because I wanted to maintain streaks and earn points. After a few weeks, it just felt natural to open it up and see what was new. It became part of my daily rhythm, even on days when I wasn't doing heavy studying.
Q5: The written materials and readings were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I also found the discussion forums surprisingly helpful because seeing other students' perspectives helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were great for keeping me engaged even when my motivation was low because they broke things into smaller, achievable goals.
Q6: I often skipped or minimized the video content, honestly. I watched over 190 minutes total, but I'd usually skim through or play them at faster speeds. I just absorb information better when I'm reading and taking notes rather than watching someone explain things. Some of the supplementary resources also got ignored because I was already overwhelmed with the core materials.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a clear connection to my assignments or exams. If something directly helped me understand what I needed to know for assessments, I was all in. The gamified elements also made things feel worthwhile because there was immediate feedback and a sense of accomplishment, which helped when my overall motivation was pretty low.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford emotionally. When my anxiety was high, I'd focus on required work and tell myself I'd get to optional stuff later. Required activities had this weight to them that forced me to engage even when I didn't want to. Optional ones were easier to postpone indefinitely, though sometimes the gamified optional tasks pulled me in anyway.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, print some out, and work through them with handwritten notes beside me. I also used external resources like academic articles and supplementary textbooks that weren't part of the LMS. My best studying often happened when I was disconnected, just me and my notes at my desk.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my typed notes, reorganizing them, or creating summary sheets from materials I'd already downloaded. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also study with a friend occasionally, quizzing each other from our notes, and that collaborative learning was completely invisible to the LMS.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a reading or resource once, download it, and then spend days working through it offline. The LMS would show one quick access, but I might have spent five or six hours with that material over the following week. This was especially true for dense readings that required multiple passes to really understand.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, usually when my anxiety spiked or when I was feeling particularly sad. Sometimes I'd look at everything I needed to do and just feel paralyzed. My attendance dropped during those times, and even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming. I had to rely on my resilience to push through, reminding myself I'd gotten through difficult periods before.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities definitely affected my engagement. When I was exhausted, I'd log in but just kind of scroll without really absorbing anything. The workload from multiple online courses sometimes meant I had to triage, focusing on what was most urgent rather than engaging deeply with everything. Confusion also slowed me down because I'd get stuck and not know how to move forward.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have the LMS open, clicking through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. My anxiety would have me going through the motions without actually processing anything. I'd finish a reading and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they looked productive on paper.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was anxiety making me avoid things that felt overwhelming. Part of it was low motivation making it hard to start. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuses. The gamified tasks actually helped break this cycle sometimes because they felt less intimidating.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator since my intrinsic motivation was low. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to engage intensely with the LMS and materials. My activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it got me through with decent results.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, especially positive feedback. When I did well on something, it gave me a boost that carried into the next task. Negative or critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me want to avoid that area for a while. The immediate feedback from gamified tasks was particularly helpful because it kept me in a loop of engagement.
Q18: The course structure with its gamified elements really helped me stay engaged despite my low motivation. Having clear modules and progression made it easier to know what to tackle next. However, the flexibility also meant I could fall behind on attendance without immediate consequences, which I sometimes took advantage of when I was struggling emotionally.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they turned studying into something almost game-like with rewards and progress tracking. That really helped when I was feeling sad or unmotivated. What made it harder was when materials weren't clearly organized or when I couldn't figure out what was most important. Dense, text-heavy sections without clear structure were particularly challenging.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking everything repeatedly without actually completing work. They might be stuck in a loop of preparation without execution, or they could be struggling to understand the material and keep reviewing without being able to produce anything. High anxiety can definitely create that pattern of activity without output.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience to some extent. My exam score was over 92, but my LMS activity might not fully explain that because so much of my studying happened offline. I'd download materials and study them extensively away from the platform. Someone could look at my attendance rate and assume I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in serious work independently.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when someone is active and what resources they're accessing, which gives a general picture of engagement timing. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone is anxiously clicking through materials without absorbing anything versus deeply engaged for a shorter period. The emotional context is invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm genuinely thinking about concepts, making connections, and wanting to learn rather than just get through it. For me, engagement also means pushing through even when I'm feeling low or anxious because I'm committed to the outcome. It's both cognitive and emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety throughout the course, or how sad I often felt while studying. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes at my desk, or the conversations with friends that helped me understand difficult concepts. It also wouldn't show the internal battle between my low motivation and my determination to succeed, or how the gamified elements helped me cope with those struggles.
